preamble.summary: Psalm 10 continues the acrostic begun in Psalm 9, completing the second half of the Hebrew alphabet. The psalm opens with a bold complaint — 'Why do You stand far off, O LORD?' — then provides the Psalter's most detailed portrait of the wicked person: arrogant, predatory, atheistic in practice, convinced God does not see. It closes with a confident petition that God will hear the afflicted, judge the oppressor, and defend the orphan and the crushed.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm contains the most sustained character study of wickedness in the Psalter. Across verses 2-11, the wicked person is profiled with psychological precision: he pursues the poor, boasts about his desires, blesses the greedy, dismisses God from his thoughts, assumes his prosperity is permanent, believes God does not see, and operates as a hidden predator — a lion in a thicket, a hunter with a net. The portrait is not of supernatural evil but of ordinary human arrogance taken to its logical end: the practical atheism of someone who lives as if there is no moral accountability.
preamble.friction: Psalm 10 has no superscription in the Hebrew text, which is unusual for Book I of the Psalter (Psalms 3-41 all have superscriptions except Psalms 1, 2, 10, and 33). This supports the view that Psalms 9 and 10 were originally one composition — Psalm 10 continues without a new heading because it is the second half of Psalm 9's acrostic. The Septuagint (Greek Old Testament) combines them as a single psalm, which shifts the numbering of all subsequent psalms by one.
preamble.connections: The acrostic structure continues from Psalm 9 (lamed through tav). The portrait of the wicked in verses 2-11 anticipates similar descriptions in Psalms 12, 14, 36, and 73. The cry 'Why do You stand far off?' echoes similar complaints in Psalms 22:2, 44:24-25, and 88:15. The petition qumah YHWH ('arise, O LORD') in verse 12 echoes Psalms 3:8, 7:7, and 9:20. The defense of the orphan and the oppressed connects to Deuteronomy 10:18 and Isaiah 1:17.

verses.text_hebrew: לָמָ֣ה יְ֭הוָה תַּעֲמֹ֣ד בְּרָח֑וֹק תַּ֝עְלִ֗ים לְעִתּ֥וֹת בַּצָּרָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Why standest thou afar off, O LORD? why hidest thou thyself in times of trouble?
verses.rendering:
Why, O LORD, do You stand far off?
Why do You hide in times of distress?
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens with the rawest question in the Psalter's first book. Lammah YHWH ta'amod be-rachoq ('why, O LORD, do You stand at a distance?') — the verb amad ('to stand') combined with be-rachoq ('at a distance, far off') pictures God as present but uninvolved, watching suffering from a safe remove. Tal'im le-ittot ba-tsarah ('You hide in times of distress') — the verb alam (hiphil, 'to hide, to conceal oneself') suggests deliberate withdrawal. God is not absent but hiding — which is worse, because it implies choice. The complaint does not deny God's existence but questions God's engagement. This is the prayer of someone who believes God is real and powerful and yet does not act. It is faith in the form of accusation.
translator_notes: The question 'why do You stand far off?' is not apostasy but the deepest form of faith — it addresses God directly, assumes God has the power to act, and demands an account of God's apparent inactivity. Only a believer complains to God about God. The atheist has no one to accuse. The lament psalms insist that honest accusation is a legitimate form of prayer.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּגַאֲוַ֣ת רָ֭שָׁע יִדְלַ֣ק עָנִ֑י יִתָּפְשׂ֓וּ ׀ בִּמְזִמּ֖וֹת ז֣וּ חָשָֽׁבוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked in his pride doth persecute the poor: let them be taken in the devices that they have imagined.
verses.rendering:
In arrogance the wicked hotly pursue the poor;
let them be caught in the schemes they have devised.
verses.expanded_rendering: The portrait of the wicked begins. Be-ga'avat rasha ('in the arrogance of the wicked') — ga'avah ('pride, arrogance, haughtiness') is the root sin. From this arrogance flows action: yidlaq ani ('he hotly pursues the poor, the afflicted'). The verb dalaq ('to burn, to pursue hotly, to chase with intensity') describes relentless, heated pursuit — the wicked burns after the poor the way fire consumes dry brush. The prayer that follows: yittaphesu bi-mezimmot zu chashuvu ('let them be caught in the schemes they have devised') — mezimmot ('schemes, plots, devices') from zamam ('to plan, to devise') describes calculated evil, not impulsive wrongdoing. The psalmist prays that the trap-makers be trapped — the same self-defeating justice of Psalms 7 and 9.
translator_notes: The word ani ('poor, afflicted') appears repeatedly in this psalm and throughout the Psalter. It does not describe only economic poverty but a condition of vulnerability — the person without resources, without power, without advocates. The wicked exploit precisely this vulnerability; God defends precisely this vulnerability.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־הִלֵּ֣ל רָ֭שָׁע עַל־תַּאֲוַ֣ת נַפְשׁ֑וֹ וּבֹצֵ֥עַ בֵּ֝רֵ֗ךְ נִ֘אֵ֥ץ ׀ יְהוָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For the wicked boasteth of his heart's desire, and blesseth the covetous, whom the LORD abhorreth.
verses.rendering:
For the wicked boasts of his soul's desire;
the greedy person blesses himself and despises the LORD.
verses.expanded_rendering: The inner life of the wicked is exposed. Hillel rasha al ta'avat nafsho ('the wicked boasts about the craving of his soul') — the verb hillel ('to boast, to praise, to celebrate') is directed at ta'avah ('craving, desire, appetite') — the wicked celebrates his own appetites, treating desire itself as praiseworthy. U-votse'a berekh ni'ets YHWH ('and the greedy one blesses himself — he despises the LORD'). Botse'a ('the greedy one, the one who cuts off profit for himself') from batsa ('to cut off, to gain unjustly') describes someone who slices profit from others for personal accumulation. This person berekh ('blesses') himself — self-congratulation replaces gratitude to God. The result: ni'ets YHWH ('he despises, scorns, treats with contempt the LORD'). The progression is: desire celebrated leads to greed practiced leads to God despised. Self-worship and God-contempt are the same movement.
translator_notes: The verb ni'ets ('to despise, to scorn, to treat with contempt') is among the strongest rejection verbs in Hebrew. It describes not passive indifference to God but active disdain — the greedy person has weighed God against profit and found God wanting. This is practical atheism expressed through economic behavior.

verses.text_hebrew: רָשָׁ֗ע כְּגֹ֣בַהּ אַ֭פּוֹ בַּל־יִדְרֹ֑שׁ אֵ֥ין אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים כׇּל־מְזִמּוֹתָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: The wicked, through the pride of his countenance, will not seek after God: God is not in all his thoughts.
verses.rendering:
The wicked, in the height of his arrogance, does not seek God.
"There is no God" — that is all his scheming.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm penetrates to the theological root of wickedness. Rasha ke-govah appo bal yidrosh ('the wicked, in the height of his face/anger, does not seek') — govah af ('height of the nose/face') is an idiom for arrogance, the lifted chin that refuses to look down or look up. Bal yidrosh ('he does not seek') — he does not inquire after God, does not investigate God's will, does not pursue relationship with God. Ein Elohim kol mezimmotav ('there is no God — all his schemes') — this is either the wicked person's internal creed ('there is no God') or the summary of his operational assumption: God is absent from all his planning. This is not philosophical atheism but practical atheism — living, planning, and scheming as if God does not exist and will never call anyone to account.
translator_notes: The phrase ein Elohim ('there is no God') appears also in Psalm 14:1 and Psalm 53:2, where it is placed in the mouth of the naval ('fool'). The denial is not intellectual but behavioral — the wicked person's life functions as a continuous declaration that God is irrelevant. Every scheme that excludes God is a repetition of ein Elohim.

verses.text_hebrew: יָחִ֤ילוּ דְרָכָ֨יו ׀ בְּכׇל־עֵ֗ת מָר֣וֹם מִ֭שְׁפָּטֶיךָ מִנֶּגְדּ֑וֹ כׇּל־צ֝וֹרְרָ֗יו יָפִ֥יחַ בָּהֶֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: His ways are always grievous; thy judgments are far above out of his sight: as for all his enemies, he puffeth at them.
verses.expanded_rendering: The wicked person's experience confirms his delusion. Yachilu derakhav be-khol et ('his ways are firm, strong, prosperous at all times') — the verb chul here may mean 'to writhe' or 'to be firm, to prosper.' The wicked person succeeds consistently, which reinforces his belief that there is no God. Marom mishpatekha mi-negdo ('Your judgments are on high, far from him, out of his sight') — God's justice is real but invisible to this person. The word marom ('height, high place') means God's judgments are above the wicked person's horizon — he cannot see them and therefore does not believe in them. Kol tsorerav yafiach bahem ('all his adversaries — he blows at them, he puffs at them') — the verb puach ('to blow, to puff, to snort') describes contemptuous dismissal, like blowing away a gnat. Enemies are beneath his notice.
translator_notes: The observation that the wicked prosper 'at all times' creates the theological problem that drives many psalms (especially Psalm 73): if God is just, why do the wicked succeed? Psalm 10 does not resolve this tension — it describes it honestly and then appeals to God to act. The honesty about wicked prosperity is itself a form of faith: the psalmist tells God the truth about what he sees.

verses.text_hebrew: אָמַ֣ר בְּ֭לִבּוֹ בַּל־אֶמּ֑וֹט לְדֹ֥ר וָ֝דֹ֗ר אֲשֶׁ֣ר לֹא־בְרָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath said in his heart, I shall not be moved: for I shall never be in adversity.
verses.rendering:
He says in his heart, "I will not be shaken;
generation after generation, no harm will touch me."
verses.expanded_rendering: The wicked person speaks to himself — amar be-libbo ('he says in his heart') reveals internal monologue, the private creed that governs his life. Bal emmot ('I will not be shaken, I will not totter') — the verb mot ('to shake, to totter, to be moved') is negated absolutely: he believes his position is permanently secure. Le-dor va-dor asher lo be-ra ('generation after generation, without evil, without calamity') extends the delusion across time — not just now but forever, not just for me but for my descendants. This is the inner voice of unchallenged privilege: the belief that current prosperity is a permanent entitlement, that the good life will never end, that one is immune to the reversals that afflict others.
translator_notes: The phrase le-dor va-dor ('generation after generation') is normally used in the Psalter for God's faithfulness (Psalm 100:5, 'His faithfulness endures to all generations'). The wicked person here co-opts the language of divine permanence for himself — he claims for his own comfort what belongs only to God's character. This is the deepest form of idolatry: treating oneself as eternal.

verses.text_hebrew: אָלָ֤ה פִּ֣יהוּ מָ֭לֵא וּמִרְמ֣וֹת וָתֹ֑ךְ תַּ֥חַת לְ֝שׁוֹנ֗וֹ עָמָ֥ל וָאָֽוֶן׃
verses.text_kjv: His mouth is full of cursing and deceit and fraud: under his tongue is mischief and vanity.
verses.rendering:
His mouth is full of cursing, deceit, and oppression;
under his tongue lurk trouble and evil.
verses.expanded_rendering: The portrait moves from inner belief to outward speech. Alah pihu male u-mirmot va-tokh ('cursing fills his mouth, along with deceit and oppression') — three forms of harmful speech. Alah ('cursing, oath-breaking, imprecation') is the abuse of solemn language. Mirmot ('deceits, frauds') from ramah ('to deceive') is deliberate lying for advantage. Tokh ('oppression, injury, fraud') describes speech that damages others. Tachat leshono amal va-aven ('under his tongue are trouble and evil') — what lurks beneath the tongue is what the person is truly preparing to say. Amal ('trouble, toil, mischief') and aven ('evil, wickedness, moral worthlessness') hide under the tongue like venom under a snake's fang, waiting to be deployed.
translator_notes: Paul quotes this verse in Romans 3:14 as part of his argument for universal human sinfulness. The image of speech as weaponry — mouth full of cursing, tongue concealing evil — reflects the Psalter's deep awareness that language is the primary instrument of both worship and harm. The same tongue that praises God can destroy neighbors.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵשֵׁ֤ב ׀ בְּמַאְרַ֬ב חֲצֵרִ֗ים בַּֽ֭מִּסְתָּרִים יַהֲרֹ֣ג נָקִ֑י עֵ֝ינָ֗יו לְחֵלְכָ֥ה יִצְפֹּֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He sitteth in the lurking places of the villages: in the secret places doth he murder the innocent: his eyes are privily set against the poor.
verses.expanded_rendering: The wicked person becomes a predator. Yeshev be-ma'rav chatserim ('he sits in ambush near the open villages') — the ma'rav ('ambush, place of lying in wait') is positioned near chatserim ('courtyards, settlements, unwalled villages') — places without walls, without defenses, where people are vulnerable. Ba-mistarim yaharog naqi ('in the hiding places he kills the innocent') — mistarim ('secret places, concealment') describes the predator's method: stealth, concealment, surprise. The naqi ('innocent, guiltless') is killed precisely because he is innocent — he is not expecting attack from the wicked. Einav le-chelkhah yitsphonu ('his eyes watch secretly for the unfortunate, the helpless') — the chelkhah (a rare word, possibly meaning 'unfortunate, wretched, helpless') is the specific target. The eyes (einayim) that should see God's judgment (v. 5) instead scan for victims.
translator_notes: The ambush imagery shifts the wicked person from economic predator (vv. 3-6) to physical predator (vv. 8-10). The progression mirrors the escalation of evil: unchecked greed leads to contempt for God, which leads to violence against the vulnerable. The psalm traces the entire trajectory from arrogance to murder.

verses.text_hebrew: יֶאֱרֹ֤ב בַּמִּסְתָּ֨ר ׀ כְּאַרְיֵ֬ה בְסֻכֹּ֗ה יֶאֱרֹב֮ לַחֲט֢וֹף עָ֫נִ֥י יַחְטֹ֣ף עָ֭נִי בְּמׇשְׁכ֣וֹ בְרִשְׁתּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: He lieth in wait secretly as a lion in his den: he lieth in wait to catch the poor: he doth catch the poor, when he draweth him into his net.
verses.expanded_rendering: The predator metaphor intensifies with two animal images. Ye'erov ba-mistar ke-aryeh be-sukkoh ('he lies in ambush in the hidden place like a lion in its lair') — the aryeh ('lion') crouches in its sukkoh ('thicket, lair, covert') waiting for prey to pass. The repetition of ye'erov ('he lies in ambush') twice in one verse mimics the patience of the hunter — waiting, waiting, then: yachtof ani ('he snatches the poor'). The verb chataf ('to snatch, to seize, to carry off') describes sudden, violent capture. Yachtof ani be-moshkho ve-rishto ('he snatches the poor by dragging him into his net') — the image shifts from lion to fisherman: the resheth ('net') catches and holds. The poor person is both prey (to the lion) and catch (in the net) — doubly trapped by a predator who uses both violence and cunning.
translator_notes: The double ambush language (ye'erov... ye'erov) creates a sense of relentless, patient stalking. The wicked person does not attack in passion but in strategy — he waits, observes, selects, and then strikes. The poor are not random victims but targeted prey, chosen for their vulnerability.

verses.text_kjv: He croucheth, and humbleth himself, that the poor may fall by his strong ones.
verses.rendering:
He crushes and crouches,
and the helpless fall into his clutches.
verses.expanded_rendering: The final image of predation. Yidakkeh yashoch ('he crushes, he crouches low') — two verbs describing the predator's posture: dakah ('to crush, to break, to press down') and shachach ('to crouch, to sink down, to bow low'). The predator lowers himself to the prey's level — not out of humility but out of strategy. The result: ve-nafal ba-atsumav chelka'im ('and the helpless fall by his strength, into his powerful grip'). The atsumav ('his strong ones, his mighty parts') may refer to the wicked person's own strength or to his agents. The chelka'im ('helpless, unfortunate, wretched ones') collapse under the weight of superior force. The psalm's portrait of the wicked is complete: from arrogance (v. 2) through atheism (v. 4) through invincibility (v. 6) through violent speech (v. 7) through ambush (v. 8-9) to final crushing victory over the helpless (v. 10).
translator_notes: The complete portrait (vv. 2-10) traces a coherent psychology of wickedness: arrogance breeds contempt for God, which breeds contempt for people, which breeds predatory violence. The psalm insists that these are not separate sins but a single trajectory — each step follows logically from the previous one.

verses.text_hebrew: אָמַ֣ר בְּ֭לִבּוֹ שָׁ֣כַח אֵ֑ל הִסְתִּ֥יר פָּ֝נָ֗יו בַּל־רָאָ֥ה לָנֶֽצַח׃
verses.text_kjv: He hath said in his heart, God hath forgotten: he hideth his face; he will never see it.
verses.rendering:
He says in his heart, "God has forgotten;
He has hidden His face; He will never see."
verses.expanded_rendering: The wicked person's final internal monologue — his theological conclusion. Amar be-libbo ('he says in his heart') — again the private creed, the secret conviction. Shakhach El ('God has forgotten') — the verb shakhach ('to forget') that was denied of God in Psalm 9:13 ('He does not forget the cry of the afflicted') is now affirmed by the wicked: God forgets. Histir panav ('He has hidden His face') — the wicked actually echoes the psalmist's own complaint from verse 1 ('why do You hide?') but draws the opposite conclusion: the psalmist sees God's hiddenness as a problem to be protested; the wicked sees it as permanent cover for his crimes. Bal ra'ah la-netsach ('He will never see, forever') — the ultimate delusion: God's apparent inaction is permanent blindness. The wicked person has constructed a theology of divine indifference to justify everything he does.
translator_notes: The irony is devastating: the wicked person says 'God will never see' in a psalm where God is called upon to see everything. The psalmist and the wicked person look at the same evidence — God's apparent inaction — and reach opposite conclusions. The psalmist says 'Why are You hiding? Act!' The wicked says 'God doesn't see. I'm free.' Same observation, opposite faith.

verses.text_hebrew: קוּמָ֤ה יְהוָ֗ה אֵ֭ל נְשָׂ֣א יָדֶ֑ךָ אַל־תִּשְׁכַּ֥ח עֲנָוִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Arise, O LORD; O God, lift up thine hand: forget not the humble.
verses.rendering:
Rise up, O LORD! O God, lift up Your hand!
Do not forget the afflicted!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm pivots from description to petition with the ancient cry: qumah YHWH ('rise up, O LORD'). El ne-sa yadekha ('O God, lift up Your hand') — the raised hand is the gesture of action, of oath-taking, of striking. The psalmist asks God to raise His hand against the wicked and in defense of the helpless. Al tishkach anavim ('do not forget the afflicted') — the verb shakhach ('to forget') directly counters the wicked person's claim in verse 11 that 'God has forgotten.' The psalmist throws the wicked person's theology back at God as prayer: the wicked says You have forgotten; I say do not forget. The entire psalm hangs on this verb — will God remember or forget?
translator_notes: The petition 'do not forget the afflicted' (al tishkach anavim) is the psalm's theological hinge. Psalm 9:13 declared that God 'does not forget the cry of the afflicted.' Psalm 10:11 quoted the wicked's claim that 'God has forgotten.' Psalm 10:12 now asks God to prove the wicked wrong by remembering. The three uses of shakhach across the two psalms form a theological argument: God's character (He does not forget) versus the wicked's delusion (God has forgotten) versus the psalmist's prayer (do not forget).

verses.text_hebrew: עַל־מֶ֤ה ׀ נִאֵ֖ץ רָשָׁ֥ע ׀ אֱלֹהִ֑ים אָמַ֥ר בְּ֝לִבּ֗וֹ לֹ֣א תִדְרֹֽשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: Wherefore doth the wicked contemn God? he hath said in his heart, Thou wilt not require it.
verses.rendering:
Why does the wicked despise God?
He says in his heart, "You will not call me to account."
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist asks God to consider what is at stake. Al meh ni'ets rasha Elohim ('on what basis, why does the wicked despise God?') — the question is not for information but for provocation. The answer follows: amar be-libbo lo tidrosh ('he says in his heart, You will not seek, You will not investigate, You will not require an account'). The verb darash ('to seek, to inquire, to investigate, to require') is the same verb used in Psalm 9:13 for God seeking out blood. The wicked has concluded that God does not investigate — does not search for evidence, does not build cases, does not prosecute. If God does not darash, then there is no accountability, and the wicked person's contempt is rational. The psalmist places the wicked person's assumption directly before God: this is what they think of You. Will You let it stand?
translator_notes: The psalmist's strategy here is theologically brilliant: by quoting the wicked person's contempt directly to God, he turns the wicked person's arrogance into a provocation of God's honor. If God does not act, the wicked person's claim — 'You will not investigate' — becomes true. The psalmist is effectively saying: Your reputation is at stake.

verses.text_hebrew: רָ֘אִ֤תָה כִּֽי־אַ֭תָּה עָמָ֣ל וָכַ֣עַס תַּבִּ֑יט לָתֵ֬ת בְּיָדֶ֗ךָ עָ֘לֶ֤יךָ יַעֲזֹ֖ב חֵלֵ֣כָה אַ֝תָּ֗ה הָיִ֤יתָ עוֹזֵ֬ר יָתֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou hast seen it; for thou beholdest mischief and spite, to requite it with thy hand: the poor committeth himself unto thee; thou art the helper of the fatherless.
verses.rendering:
But You do see! You observe trouble and grief
to take the matter into Your own hand.
The helpless entrust themselves to You;
You are the helper of the orphan.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's theological turning point. Ra'itah ('You have seen!') — a direct contradiction of the wicked's claim in verse 11 ('He will never see'). The psalmist declares with certainty: God sees. Ki attah amal va-kha'as tabbit ('for You observe trouble and grief') — the verb nabat ('to look, to observe, to gaze intently') describes not casual glancing but purposeful examination. Latet beyadekha ('to take it into Your hand, to deal with it personally') — God's hand (yad) receives the matter for action. Alekha ya'azov chelkhah ('the helpless one entrusts himself to You, abandons himself to You') — the verb azav here means not 'to abandon' but 'to commit, to entrust' — the helpless person has no one else and so places everything in God's care. Attah hayita ozer yatom ('You have been the helper of the orphan') — the yatom ('orphan, fatherless child') is the paradigmatic vulnerable person in the ancient world. God's identity is bound to the defense of orphans.
translator_notes: The orphan (yatom) appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as the test case for justice. A society's treatment of orphans reveals its moral character. God's self-identification as 'helper of the orphan' (ozer yatom) establishes a divine priority: those without human protectors have a divine one. Deuteronomy 10:18 says God 'executes justice for the orphan and the widow.' Psalm 68:6 calls God 'father of the fatherless.'

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁ֘בֹ֤ר זְר֣וֹעַ רָ֭שָׁע וָ֗רָע תִּדְרוֹשׁ־רִשְׁע֥וֹ בַל־תִּמְצָֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: Break thou the arm of the wicked and the evil man: seek out his wickedness till thou find none.
verses.rendering:
Break the arm of the wicked and the evildoer;
search out his wickedness until none remains.
verses.expanded_rendering: The petition becomes specific and fierce. Shevor zero'a rasha va-ra ('break the arm of the wicked and the evil person') — the zero'a ('arm') represents power, the capacity to act with force. To break the arm is to strip the oppressor of his ability to oppress. This is not a prayer for the wicked person's suffering but for the disabling of his power. Tidrosh rish'o bal timtsa ('search out his wickedness until You find none') — the verb darash ('to seek, to investigate') returns: the wicked said God would not investigate (v. 13); the psalmist now asks God to investigate so thoroughly that no wickedness remains undiscovered. Bal timtsa ('until You find none, until it cannot be found') envisions complete eradication — every pocket of evil exposed and eliminated.
translator_notes: The prayer to 'break the arm' of the wicked is not vindictive cruelty but a petition for the removal of oppressive power. The arm is the instrument of violence; breaking it ends the violence. The psalmist does not ask for the wicked person's torture but for the termination of his capacity to harm.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֣ה מֶ֭לֶךְ עוֹלָ֣ם וָעֶ֑ד אָבְד֥וּ ג֝וֹיִ֗ם מֵאַרְצֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is King for ever and ever: the heathen are perished out of his land.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is king forever and ever;
the nations have perished from His land.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's central confession: YHWH melekh olam va-ed ('the LORD is king forever and ever'). This is the first explicit declaration of God's kingship in the Psalter using the title melekh ('king'). Against the wicked person who claimed permanence for himself (v. 6, 'generation after generation'), the psalm sets God's actual permanence: olam va-ed ('forever and ever'). The consequence: av'du goyim me-artso ('the nations have perished from His land'). Artso ('His land') — the earth belongs to God, and those who defy its owner will eventually be removed. The nations' power is temporary; God's reign is eternal. The practical atheist's confidence — 'I will never be shaken' — is overturned by the reality of a King who never ceases to reign.
translator_notes: The declaration YHWH melekh ('the LORD is king') anticipates the great enthronement psalms (93, 95-99). It is the Psalter's foundational political theology: all earthly power is subordinate to divine sovereignty. The nations may rage (Psalm 2), the wicked may prosper (Psalm 10), but the LORD remains king. This confession is the Psalter's answer to every form of injustice.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּאֲוַ֬ת עֲנָוִ֣ים שָׁמַ֣עְתָּ יְהוָ֑ה תָּכִ֥ין לִ֝בָּ֗ם תַּקְשִׁ֥יב אׇזְנֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: LORD, thou hast heard the desire of the humble: thou wilt prepare their heart, thou wilt cause thine ear to hear:
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm approaches its conclusion with confident declarations about God's attention to the vulnerable. Ta'avat anavim shama'ta YHWH ('the desire of the afflicted You have heard, O LORD') — the ta'avah ('desire, longing') of the anavim is heard by God. The wicked boasted about his own ta'avah (v. 3); God hears the ta'avah of the afflicted. Takhin libbam ('You prepare, establish, steady their hearts') — the verb kun ('to establish, to make firm') is applied to the hearts of the vulnerable: God gives inner stability to those whose outer circumstances are unstable. Taqshiv oznekha ('You incline Your ear') — the divine ear bends toward the afflicted. Every component of this verse directly contradicts the wicked person's theology: God has not forgotten (v. 11), God does see (v. 14), and God does hear (v. 17).
translator_notes: The phrase takhin libbam ('You steady their hearts') reveals something important about God's care for the afflicted: it is not only external deliverance but internal strengthening. God does not merely rescue the vulnerable from their circumstances but fortifies their inner lives. The heart (lev) that has been shaken by oppression is made firm (kun) by God.

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁפֹּ֥ט יָת֗וֹם וָ֫דָ֥ךְ בַּל־י֘וֹסִ֤יף ע֗וֹד לַעֲרֹ֥ץ אֱנ֥וֹשׁ מִן־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: To judge the fatherless and the oppressed, that the man of the earth may no more oppress.
verses.rendering:
to defend the orphan and the crushed,
so that mere mortals from the earth
may terrify no more.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse states God's judicial purpose with precision. Lishpot yatom va-dakh ('to judge, to defend, to vindicate the orphan and the crushed') — shophet here means not 'to condemn' but 'to defend, to set right, to deliver justice for.' The yatom ('orphan') from verse 14 and the dakh ('crushed, oppressed') from Psalm 9:10 are the psalm's representative vulnerable persons. The purpose of God's intervention: bal yosif od la'arots enosh min ha-arets ('so that a mortal from the earth may no longer cause terror'). The verb arats ('to terrify, to inspire dread, to oppress through fear') describes what the wicked do to the powerless. The word enosh ('mortal, frail human') returns from Psalm 9:20-21 — the same word that reminds us the oppressor is merely human. Min ha-arets ('from the earth') emphasizes earthly origin: the one who terrifies is himself nothing more than dust from the ground. The psalm ends where Psalm 9 ended: with the demand that mortals be recognized as mortals, that human power be seen for what it is — temporary, derivative, and subject to the King who reigns forever.
translator_notes: The final line — 'so that mere mortals from the earth may terrify no more' — is the Psalter's most concentrated statement against human tyranny. The tyrant is reduced to his essential identity: enosh min ha-arets ('a mortal from the earth, a creature of dust'). When God arises to defend the orphan and the crushed, the pretension of human power is exposed as the fraud it always was. The oppressor who claimed God would never see is himself seen, judged, and stripped of his ability to terrorize.
